data.title: Measles case confirmed at Clemson amid South Carolina outbreak
data.description:
(NewsNation) — A case of measles has been reported at Clemson University as South Carolina grapples with one of the worst outbreaks of the disease in the U.S.

The South Carolina Department of Public Health confirmed “an individual affiliated with the university” has tested positive for the highly contagious disease.

“The individual has isolated, per DPH requirements, and DPH is conducting contact tracing with individuals who may have been exposed and outlining isolation and quarantine protocols,” the agency said.

Health officials didn’t release additional details about the case. Those believed to have been exposed will be notified by email with instructions on quarantining.

According to Clemson Student Health Services, nearly 98 percent of students on the main campus have provided proof of measles immunity.

“The health, safety, and well-being of Clemson’s campus community remains our highest priority,” the university said, noting that guidance was provided to students, faculty, and staff regarding measles preparedness.

The case comes as South Carolina health officials confirm at least 124 measles cases, bringing the state’s total since the outbreak began last fall to 558.

There are at least 171 confirmed measles cases across nine states, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

Measles was declared eliminated in the U.S. in 2000, but cases have resurged, putting the country at risk of losing that status.

The rise in measles cases coincides with efforts by Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. to downplay the severity of the disease and the importance of vaccination.

data.title: Recalled tuna accidently shipped to stores in 9 states: How to know if your cans are impacted
data.description:
(WJW) – The U.S. Food and Drug Administration is warning consumers that previously recalled canned tuna was recently shipped to retail stores in several states.

According to a new recall alert shared by the FDA on Monday, a third-party distributor “inadvertently” shipped quarantined canned tuna that Tri-Union Seafoods recalled roughly a year ago.

Officials said the initial recall was issued after the company learned that some of the product’s “easy open” pull tab lids were defective and could cause the cans to leak, “or worse, be contaminated with clostridium botulinum, a potentially fatal form of food poisoning.”

Clostridium botulinum can create a toxin that causes botulism, the U.S. Department of Agriculture explains. The disease can lead to trouble breathing, muscle paralysis, and, in serious cases, death, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Foods that have been improperly canned, preserved, or fermented are known to create the right conditions for the toxin to form.

More recently, a widespread outbreak of infant botulism was linked to recalled baby formula.

The recalled tuna was distributed to specific stores in a handful of states:

  • Meijer: Illinois, Indiana, Kentucky, Michigan, Ohio, and Wisconsin
  • Giant Foods: Maryland and Virginia
  • Safeway, Albertsons, Vons, and Pavilions: California

The cans also bear specific UPC and can codes, as well as best-by dates:

The recall notice from the FDA included the below images of the packaging:

If you have any cans of tuna matching the above descriptions, regulators advise you to not eat it, regardless of whether it looks or smells spoiled. If you have eaten any of the recalled tuna and begin to feel unwell, it’s recommended that you seek immediate medical care.

Cans of tuna impacted by the recall can be returned to the Meijer, Giant Foods, Safeway, Albertsons, Vons, or Pavilions store where they were purchased for a full refund. The FDA notice says customers can also throw the can away or “contact Tri-Union Seafoods directly for a retrieval kit and a coupon for a replacement product.”

data.title: Greene says Trump's campaign endorsements 'solidify the swamp'
data.description:
Former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.) — who left Congress this month after a bitter spat with the president — said President Trump’s campaign endorsements undermine his purported efforts to “drain the swamp.”

In a post Tuesday on the social platform X, the Georgia Republican urged the public to evaluate potential candidates based on their track record, not just whether they have earned Trump’s endorsement.

“Trump’s endorsements do not drain the swamp, his endorsements solidify the swamp and ensure the swamp is never drained,” she wrote on X.

“Keep voting for Trump endorsed candidates with F voting records and you are responsible for never draining the swamp. Like it or not, that’s the truth,” she added.

Greene pushed back on suggestions that the president’s endorsements are determined by independent staff, saying, “he is not being deceived by his team. It’s not sabotage when he chooses the candidates and controls the agenda.”

Responding to a comment saying Trump’s “endorsement team is awful,” Greene wrote, “Yes the team is uniparty and donor driven, but it ain’t the team, it’s him.”

“He’s bff’s with Lindsey Graham for the love of god,” Greene continued, referring to the longtime South Carolina Republican senator. “No one makes Trump do anything.”

Party leaders, in elections, typically back incumbent candidates, who historically have had the best chance of winning in a primary or general election.

But Greene suggested Trump could “drain the swamp” and pass his preferred policies without interference from Congress if he backed challengers trying to unseat those politicians.

She shared a post that said, “Instead, he endorses against the challengers that are coming to serve as the cavalry and be reinforcements for him.”

Greene, who was long a fierce Trump supporter, said she no longer identifies as part of the MAGA movement after she broke with the president over his handling of the Epstein documents and expiring health care subsidies.

Trump lashed out against her public criticism, calling her a “traitor” and rescinding his endorsement of the congresswoman, who announced her decision to leave Congress shortly thereafter.

data.title: Europe cannot be a 'miserable slave' to Trump: Belgian PM
data.description:
Belgian Prime Minister Bart De Wever said Europe is “under a lot of pressure” from the Trump administration’s tariffs and recent threats to overtake Greenland, while issuing a strong warning to European leaders not to cave to these demands. 

The politician, a member of the conservative Flemish nationalist N-VA party, made the comments during a panel discussion at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, on Tuesday. 

De Wever said Europe is “at a crossroads where it has to decide” to either unite or “explode in any possible direction.” 

“Until now, we tried to appease the new president in the White House,” De Wever said. “We were very lenient, also with the tariffs, we were lenient, hoping to get his support for the Ukraine war. 

“We were in a very bad position at the moment, we were dependent on the United States, so we chose to be lenient, but now so many red lines are being crossed that you have the choice between your self respect,” he continued. “Being a happy vessel is one thing, being a miserable slave is something else. If you back down now, you’re going to lose your dignity, and that’s probably the most precious thing you can have in a democracy is your dignity.”

De Wever said that he and Belgian King Philippe are scheduled to meet President Trump on Wednesday, when the U.S. president joins the conference. 

The prime minister noted that the meeting between the Belgian and American leaders had been arranged prior to “the Greenland issue” and that now, “it will have a different character than we had planned.” De Wever said he plans to tell Trump, “You’re crossing red lines here.”

“We either stand together, or we will stand divided, and if we are divided, there is the end of an era of 80 years of Atlantism really drawing to a close,” he said. 

The Belgian prime minister also paraphrased Italian Marxist philosopher Antonio Gramsci, saying, “If the old is dying and the new is not yet born, then you live in a time of monsters.”

“It’s up to [Trump] to decide if he wants to be a monster, yes or no,” De Wever said.

data.title: Former Trump aide on threatening allies over Greenland: 'Mentally ill, deranged'
data.description:
A former aide to President Trump from his first term, who broke with the White House over its handling of the Jan. 6, 2021 attack at the Capitol, on Tuesday berated his push to annex Greenland.

“Yeah, I don’t say this lightly. I think this might be the most mentally ill, deranged thing that Donald Trump has done to date, to be threatening our allies and wanting to go to war, potentially over Greenland, when his reasoning doesn’t even make sense,” former White House deputy press secretary Sarah Matthews said during a Tuesday appearance MS NOW’s “The Weeknight.”

“He says we need Greenland because Russia and China will take over Greenland if we don’t. But then he invites [Russian President] Vladimir Putin to sit on his [Gaza] Board of Peace. Make it make sense for me because it doesn’t make any sense,” she added.

The Trump administration argues Greenland is essential to “national security purposes” and has bucked NATO allies for defending the island’s self autonomy. 

On Saturday, Trump announced a 10 percent tax on Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the United Kingdom, the Netherlands and Finland set to go into effect on Feb. 1 and increase to 25 percent on June 1. The tariffs would be imposed, per Trump, unless the countries accede to U.S. control of Greenland,.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent urged countries not to retaliate but to speak with the president during the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland later this week.

“Everyone, take a deep breath. Do not escalate, do not escalate. And President Trump has a strategy here,” Bessent said during an appearance on CNBC.

“Hear him out, and then everything will be fine,” the Treasury chief added.

Matthews, echoing other critics of the Trump push, said the actions are detrimental to U.S. alliances.

“He’s alienating our allies. And I think the voters who voted for him are really frustrated because people did not vote for him to take over Greenland,” the former deputy press secretary said.

European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen echoed her sentiments.

“The European Union and the United States have agreed to a trade deal last July,” Von der Leyen said on Tuesday at the World Economic Forum. “And in politics as in business – a deal is a deal. And when friends shake hands, it must mean something.”

data.title: Mamdani says he backs abolishing ICE
data.description:
New York City Mayor (D) Zohran Mamdani said Tuesday he supports getting rid of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) as the Trump administration ramps up its crackdown on illegal immigration.

“I am in support of abolishing ICE and I’ll tell you why. Because what we see is an entity that has no interest in fulfilling its stated reason to exist,” Mamdani said on ABC’s “The View.”

“We’re seeing a government agency that is supposed to be enforcing some kind of an immigration law, but instead what’s it’s doing is terrorizing people no matter their immigration status, no matter the facts of the law, no matter the facts of the case,” he added.

The Trump administration has surged ICE and Customs and Border Protection personnel to Democratic-run cities across the nation, including New York City, Los Angeles and Chicago. The moves have been heavily scrutinized by Democratic lawmakers and protesters, most recently in the wake of an ICE officer fatally shooting Renee Good in Minneapolis earlier this month. 

An Economist/YouGov poll released last week found that 46 percent of respondents support abolishing the agency, with 43 percent opposed to getting rid of the agency.

“I’m tired of waking up every day and seeing a new image of someone being dragged out of a car, dragged out of their home, dragged out of their life,” Mamdani said Tuesday. “What we need to see is humanity, and there is a way to care about immigration in this city and in this country with a sense of humanity. 

“What we’re seeing from ICE is not it and we have not seen that from them in a long, long time,” the New York City mayor added.

He also criticized ICE during his mayoral campaign, telling Zeteo’s Mehdi Hasan last June that it is a “rogue agency” and that “when we think about what this country needs, and a humane immigration policy that has some thought and care for law and order, that clearly is not ICE.”

After he was elected, Mamdani released a video advising migrants on what to do if federal immigration enforcement officers confronted them, noting that individuals have the right to remain silent, ask whether they are free to go while being and film agency operations so long as they do not interfere with an arrest.

“If ICE does not have a judicial warrant signed by a judge, you have the right to say, ‘I do not consent to entry,’ and the right to keep your door closed,” he said in the video. 

Last week, Mamdani also said he was “outraged” by federal immigration officers detaining a New York City Council employee.

New York City Council Speaker Julie Menin wrote in a statement that the employee, who was of Venezuelan descent and obtained legal authorization to remain in the country in October, was at a “routine immigration appointment” on Long Island when authorities picked him up.

“We are doing everything we can to secure his immediate release, and we demand swift and transparent action by the federal government on this apparent overreach,” Menin added.

Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) and Rep. Shri Thanedar (D-Mich.) have also pushed for ICE’s abolishment. Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D), however, said he does not support shuttering the agency.

data.title: Trump 2.0 first year foreign policy: An intriguing mixed bag
data.description:
President Trump’s second term marks one of the most activist foreign policy presidencies in history, from tariff wars to toppling dictators and much in between. Simply turning on the news has become something of an adventure — you never know what frantic headline to expect next or from where.

Unfortunately, nearly all assessments of Trump 47’s foreign policy have focused on shattered norms (especially free trade), disregarded precedents (alliance relations), and ignored guardrails (domestic checks on executive power) to conclude, as does the New York Times, that irreparable damage is being done to America and the U.S.-led “rules-based world.”

But hyper-focusing on Trump’s disruptive and often destructive style inhibits an ability to properly judge which of his radical policy shifts are beneficial, and which are not.

Many of Trump’s policies — including his hardline posture toward Iran and renewed focus on military power — were urgently needed and will likely stand the test of time. Others, including his scattershot approach to tariffs and reckless stance toward allies, face a murkier legacy.

But even on the murkier issues, Trump deserves credit for shaking up status quos that had grown unsustainable. That includes a global trade system designed exclusively for the liberal-capitalist West, and alliances built for the Cold War. With a few further adjustments, Trump may one day be viewed as the president who reset America’s flailing post-Cold War foreign policy and reinvigorated its global leadership.

The wild card, of course, is Trump himself, who can turn positives into negatives at the speed of a Truth Social post.

Where that leaves us one year into Trump’s second term is a decidedly mixed bag, but with greater upside than down. The challenge moving forward will be to bolster what’s working, fine tune what needs adjusting, and prevent worst-case scenarios of Trump-induced self-destruction.

It is difficult to overstate how beleaguered American power projection had become by the time Trump 47 entered office. The Biden administration’s catastrophic Afghanistan withdrawal in 2021 was followed in quick succession by Russia’s 2022 invasion of Ukraine and Iran’s proxy-led war against Israel in 2023. During this period, Chinese President Xi Jinping felt so emboldened he forged a “no limits” partnership with Russian President Vladimir Putin and instructed the People’s Liberation Army to be ready to invade Taiwan by 2027.

In contrast, Trump has made the credible threat of U.S. military action central to world politics. His first term included a decapitation strike against a leader of Iran’s Revolutionary Guard Corps and retaliatory strikes against Syria’s al-Assad regime for gassing civilians — something the prior Obama administration famously declined to do. He also sent lethal military aid to Ukraine, which the Obama-Biden White House had considered too provocative toward Russia.

Trump’s second term has maintained focus on hard power to achieve strategic aims, including strikes against Iran’s nuclear program and the daring apprehension of Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro. The world must account for a credible U.S. military deterrent not seen since Ronald Reagan. And like Reagan, Trump has proposed major increases in defense spending to meet our “dangerous times.”

Trump is certainly correct that global trade requires a major reset. The World Trade Organization was created when neo-liberal economic theory was ascendent, and assumed China’s entry would embolden peace. Instead, it ushered a new era of great power competition over strategic resources and advanced technologies, requiring a more realistic approach to trade and manufacturing.

Trump is the first president willing to act on this, and there is now an opportunity to create new multilateral trade organizations that safeguard both free trade competition and national security.

On alliances, Trump’s hot rhetoric has mostly outpaced his actions. In the first term, he forged the QUAD security pact with Australia, India and Japan to counter China in the Indo-Pacific. He has now convinced NATO countries to boost their defense spending. But he still muses about not defending NATO allies, and his tariff policies have harmed allies in both the QUAD and NATO.

Such tension over “permanent alliances” dates back to George Washington. While America’s alliance systems served it well during the Cold War, the present era requires a new assessment of cost, commitment and needs.

The greatest threat to a Trump-inspired realignment of American foreign policy is, of course, Trump himself. Though rumors of the death of American democracy under Trump have been greatly exaggerated, his autocratic impulses always pose a serious risk of undermining any policy benefits (or worse).

Fortunately, the American system is built to contain an imperial-minded leader, while still permitting healthy policy innovation. The Supreme Court is reviewing multiple cases regarding presidential authority over national security and trade. It has issued rulings against the wishes of Trump and will likely issue more.

Congress retains the ultimate ability to rein in executive overreach through its sole power over the purse. And upcoming elections guarantee a healthy debate over Trump’s foreign policy, putting power into the hands of voters to determine the shape of future policy.

In other words, Trump 47’s foreign policy initiatives will likely be judged on their merits, and as part of a much-needed reevaluation of America’s proper role in the world.

Stuart Gottlieb teaches American foreign policy and international security at Columbia University. He previously served as a foreign policy adviser and speechwriter in the Senate from 1999 to 2003.

data.title: Gallego on Trump Greenland takeover push: 'He is a madman'
data.description:
Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) on Monday took a swing at President Trump over his quest to acquire Greenland, saying the president “is not rational right now.”

“Well, it seems like European leaders’ strategy so far has been to basically try to deescalate, reason with the president, find some kind of an off-ramp. Do you think there is an off-ramp?” CNN’s Anderson Cooper asked Gallego on his show.

“No, and I’ve been very clear. He is a madman. He is insane. He’s only thinking about himself,“ Gallego responded, in comments highlighted by Mediaite.

“And you know that he’s insane?” Cooper asked.

“Yes, I’m sorry, where are we at this moment where we don’t understand what’s happening in this country? The man is threatening war against a NATO ally. We all think this is rational, right? Let’s accept what’s happening here. He is not rational right now,” the Arizona senator replied.

“He is destroying our world reputation or potentially our economic opportunity or economic mighty power around the world because he is being petty. None of this is rational. Everyone needs to stop pretending this is rational,” he added.

Throughout the first year of his second term, Trump’s foreign policy actions have strained relationships with allies and adversaries alike. Trump has sought to take over Greenland for months, but his threats to do so have ramped up recently. The Arctic island is a semiautonomous territory of Denmark, a NATO ally of the U.S.

Trump has suggested the Norwegian Nobel Committee’s pick of Venezuelan opposition leader María Corina Machado for last year’s Nobel Peace Prize was a reason behind his push for Greenland.

California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) on Tuesday advised Europe to “buck up” amid Trump’s Greenland threats.

“Do you have a message for Europeans who are concerned about the messages from the White House around Greenland this week?” Sky News’s Paul Kelso asked Newsom.

“Yeah, it’s time to buck up,” Newsom replied. “It’s time to get serious. And stop being complicit. It’s time to stand tall and firm, have a backbone. You know, I’ve seen this in the United States, supine Congress, playing both sides. You know, say one thing on a text or a tweet, another publicly.”

The Hill has reached out to the White House for comment.

data.title: Sherrill inaugurated as New Jersey governor, draws 'parallels' with American Revolution
data.description:
New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill (D) drew parallels between the current political moment and the American Revolution on Tuesday as she was sworn in as governor of the Garden State.

“The list of grievances in our Declaration of Independence included these charges against the king: He has refused his assent to laws. He has obstructed the administration of justice. He has made judges dependent on his will alone, and yes, he has kept among us, in times of peace, standing armies without the consent of our legislatures,” Sherrill said in an inaugural address that underscored New Jersey’s role in the Revolutionary War.

“This election proved that the people of New Jersey recognize the parallels: That we see a president illegally usurping power, unconstitutionally enacting a tariff regime to make billions for himself and his family while everyone else sees their costs go higher,” the newly sworn-in governor said in an apparent nod to President Trump.

“And on November 4, the people of New Jersey once again gave the nation a glimmer of something it hadn’t felt in months: hope. Yes, 250 years after the Declaration … once again, New Jersey is leading the way,” she said to applause.  

Sherrill, a four-term congresswoman and former Navy helicopter pilot, succeeds former Gov. Phil Murphy (D) as the state’s 57th governor and the second woman to lead the state. 

Her decisive victory over a Trump-endorsed rival marked a major win for Democrats after the party’s bruising 2024 losses, with the 2025 election results seen as a referendum on the president and his administration. 

Virginia Gov. Abigail Spanberger (D), who also clinched a key off-year election win alongside Sherrill, was inaugurated over the weekend. 

Sherrill on Tuesday took the oath of office on New Jersey’s original parchment copy of the Constitution, one of 13 known to still be in existence.

She also signed two executive orders during her inaugural speech, declaring a state of emergency on utility costs in the state. One order freezes utility rate hikes, and another aims for new sources of electricity production with the goal of lowering utility costs long-term.

data.title: Nothing but mourning for the anniversary of Trump 2.0
data.description:
The first anniversary of President Trump’s second term marks a year of broken promises, personal vendettas and failed policies. The nexus of Trump’s malpractice on so many fronts is his failure to fix the economy. 

He promised many times during the 2024 presidential campaign to bring prices down on the first day of his second term. The cost of living increased by an annualized 2.7 percent last month. His failure to honor his pledge has cost him the trust of Americans as they find it harder to feed their families and pay for the insurance which preserves their health and wellbeing.  

A study by the Center for American Progress reveals that Trump’s political base of Americans with low education levels have been hit especially hard by his malfeasance. Vulnerable Republican congressional candidates and the Republican nominee for president in 2028 will be the ones to suffer for their dear leader’s failure to capitalize on his in 2024 gains.

The biggest Trump problem is his misplaced policy priorities. He also promised to put America first. Instead of fixing the economy at home, he has focused like a laser beam on his imperial ambitions abroad. The proof is his obsession with Greenland, which belongs to our NATO ally Denmark. He has promised to conquer it by any means necessary and won’t even rule out an invasion.

Our European allies have rebuffed his drive to take over the island. Some even sent ships and soldiers to resist a Trump invasion. In a fit of pique, he took his vengeance on Americans by raising tariffs on goods imported for from those nations. This tax will ultimately pass costs on to consumers. U.S. citizens will pay a heavy price for his outsized foreign misadventures. 

A new national survey by CNN pronounces the public verdict on the tragic first year of Trump’s troubled second term. His ratings are remarkably and consistently negative. The sad state of the economy is the public’s single biggest concern, and the body politic finds his performance on the problem grossly inadequate.

Roughly three out of every five Americans believe Trump’s presidency has been a failure. About the same share of the public believes he hasn’t focused on the most important problems facing the nation and, accordingly, disapproves of his performance on the economy. Republicans should be running scared, distancing themselves from Trump as fast and as far as they can.

The political backlash has been enormous. Democrats lead the generic congressional head-to-head by 5 percentage points. Even more ominous for Republicans is the intensity of the Trump opposition. Democrats are 16 points more likely than Republicans to say that they’re “extremely motivated” to vote in the midterm elections. Trump’s name may not be on the ballot this year, but it is lodged in the hearts and minds of the electorate. 

A recent Gallup survey indicates that every Trump action creates a positive reaction for Democrats. This conservative president has done more than anyone since Franklin Delano Roosevelt to create new liberals. The proportion of Americans (and of Democrats) who identify themselves as liberal is at a 50-year high. Democrats have an 8-point advantage in party identification over Republicans. 

It has been a year since Trump rose to take his second oath of office. It’s been all downhill for him and the nation since that fateful day. My Democrats have their own image problems to fix. But on the anniversary of his second swearing-in, we should be thankful that The Donald is his own worst enemy and cannot get out of his own way. 

Brad Bannon is a national Democratic strategist and CEO of Bannon Communications. He writes weekly for The Hill and hosts the popular progressive podcast on power, politics and policy, Deadline D.C. with Brad Bannon.
